pattern: Recursive spatial subdivision becomes canonical only when you merge on the way back up: build the four quadrants first, then fuse them into a single leaf whenever all four agree.
hints: Split the square into four half-size quadrants and solve each one the same way — the recursion bottoms out at a single cell, which is always a leaf.
Checking whether a whole region is uniform up front works, but you can avoid rescanning by deciding after the four recursive calls return.
If all four children came back as leaves carrying the same value, throw them away and return one leaf with that value instead of an internal node.

edge_inputs.description: 2x2 checkerboard that cannot collapse at all
4x4 grid that is entirely uniform — must come back as one leaf, not a tree of identical leaves
4x4 grid where three quadrants collapse to leaves but the bottom-left stays internal
uniform except a single flipped cell in the bottom-right corner, forcing maximum depth along exactly one path
8x8 grid whose top half is solid and bottom half mixes uniform and non-uniform 4x4 quadrants
4x4 grid where each 2x2 sub-block is uniform yet no two quadrants share a value, so nothing above the leaves may merge

stress.description: power-of-two grids painted with a base value plus a few random rectangles so uniform regions and collapses actually occur
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 1 << rng.randint(0, 6)
    base = rng.randint(0, 1)
    grid = [[base] * n for _ in range(n)]
    for _ in range(rng.randint(0, 6)):
        r1 = rng.randrange(n)
        r2 = rng.randint(r1, n - 1)
        c1 = rng.randrange(n)
        c2 = rng.randint(c1, n - 1)
        v = rng.randint(0, 1)
        for r in range(r1, r2 + 1):
            for c in range(c1, c2 + 1):
                grid[r][c] = v
    return [grid]
